questions.explanation: WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) is the international standard for web accessibility published by the W3C. The other options are incorrect acronyms unrelated to accessibility standards.

questions.q: Why is keyboard navigation important for web accessibility?
options: Some users cannot or prefer not to use a mouse; keyboard navigation allows them to access all functionality
questions.explanation: Many users with motor disabilities, blindness, or repetitive strain injuries rely on keyboards. Keyboard-accessible sites serve all users regardless of input device. Keyboard access doesn't inherently affect speed, server load, or aesthetics.

questions.q: What is ARIA in the context of web accessibility?
options: Accessible Rich Internet Applications; a set of attributes that improves accessibility of dynamic content and custom widgets
questions.explanation: ARIA attributes help assistive technologies understand dynamic content and complex widgets by providing semantic information. ARIA is not a color framework, server type, or operating system.

questions.q: What is the minimum contrast ratio recommended by WCAG AA for normal text?
questions.explanation: WCAG AA requires a 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text to ensure legibility for users with low vision. The 7:1 ratio applies to AAA level, while 2:1 and 3:1 are below recommended standards.

questions.q: Why should links not be identified by color alone?
options: Colorblind users and those viewing in grayscale cannot distinguish links by color only; links should have additional visual or semantic indicators
questions.explanation: Approximately 8% of men have color vision deficiency; links must be distinguishable without relying on color alone, such as through underlines or different text styling. Color itself is not the problem; using it as the only identifier is.

questions.q: How do screen readers help users with blindness navigate web content?
options: They convert text and semantic information into speech or braille output, allowing users to hear or feel page content
They add background colors to text
questions.explanation: Screen readers analyze page structure and text, then output content through speakers or braille displays. Magnification, color addition, and image removal are separate accessibility features, not screen reader functions.

questions.q: What should be included in alternative text (alt text) for images?
options: A description of the image's file size
A concise description of the image content and its purpose in context, so screen reader users understand the information conveyed
questions.explanation: Alt text should convey the image's meaning and purpose. File size, photographer credits, and generic labels don't serve blind users. Good alt text varies by context; decorative images can have empty alt="" attributes.

questions.q: Why should focus indicators (like outline boxes around buttons) never be removed?
options: They are required by law in all countries
Keyboard users rely on visible focus indicators to know which element they're currently interacting with; removing them causes disorientation and errors
questions.explanation: Focus indicators show keyboard navigators where they are in the interface. Removing them makes navigation confusing and frustrating. While legal requirements vary, usability demands visible focus. Indicators don't impact aesthetics or performance.

questions.q: What does 'perceivable' mean as a WCAG principle?
options: Information that only experts can understand
Users must be able to perceive all information; information cannot be invisible to all senses
Information that moves on the page
Information that requires JavaScript to load
questions.explanation: The WCAG principle of perceivable requires that information be presented in ways users can see, hear, or feel—not invisible to all senses. Perceivable content doesn't require expertise, animation, or JavaScript.

questions.q: How should a designer ensure form fields are accessible?
options: Use only visual labels without associated code
Associate explicit labels with form controls using proper HTML, maintain keyboard accessibility, provide clear error messages, and ensure adequate color contrast
Use very large fonts for all fields
Require only mouse input to submit forms
questions.explanation: Accessible forms require proper semantic HTML labels, keyboard support, clear errors, and sufficient contrast. Visual labels alone don't help screen readers; large fonts, and mouse-only submission harm usability. Proper association of labels is fundamental.
